questions.title: Purpose of a primary key
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of a primary key in a relational table?
questions.explanation: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a table and cannot contain NULL values.

options.text: To uniquely identify each row in the table
To store the largest column value

questions.title: What SELECT does
questions.description: Which SQL statement is used to retrieve data from a table?
questions.explanation: SELECT is the read operation in SQL; it returns rows matching the query without modifying the data.

questions.title: Role of a foreign key
questions.description: What does a foreign key enforce between two tables?
questions.explanation: A foreign key enforces referential integrity by ensuring a value in one table matches a primary/unique key in another.

questions.title: Purpose of WHERE clause
questions.description: What is the WHERE clause used for in a SQL query?
questions.explanation: The WHERE clause filters rows, returning only those that satisfy the given condition.

options.text: To filter rows based on a condition

questions.title: What an index improves
questions.description: What is the main benefit of adding an index to a column?
questions.explanation: An index speeds up read queries that filter or sort on the indexed column, at the cost of slower writes and extra storage.

options.text: Faster lookups on the indexed column

questions.title: INSERT statement purpose
questions.description: Which statement adds a new row to a table?
questions.explanation: INSERT adds new rows of data into a table.

questions.title: DELETE vs DROP
questions.description: What is the difference between DELETE and DROP?
questions.explanation: DELETE removes rows from a table; DROP removes the entire table (or object) including its structure.

options.text: DELETE removes rows; DROP removes the whole table
DELETE removes the table; DROP removes rows

questions.title: Purpose of ORDER BY
questions.description: What does the ORDER BY clause do?
questions.explanation: ORDER BY sorts the result set by one or more columns, ascending by default.

options.text: Sorts the result set by specified columns
Limits the number of returned rows

questions.title: What NULL represents
questions.description: In SQL, what does NULL represent?
questions.explanation: NULL represents an unknown or missing value; it is not the same as zero or an empty string.

questions.title: Purpose of COUNT
questions.description: What does the aggregate function COUNT(*) return?
questions.explanation: COUNT(*) returns the number of rows that match the query.

options.text: The largest value in a column

questions.title: What a relational database stores data in
questions.description: In a relational database, data is organized primarily into what structure?
questions.explanation: Relational databases store data in tables made up of rows and columns.

questions.title: Purpose of UPDATE
questions.description: Which statement modifies existing rows in a table?
questions.explanation: UPDATE changes values in existing rows, usually combined with a WHERE clause to target specific rows.

questions.title: What DISTINCT does
questions.description: What is the effect of the DISTINCT keyword in a SELECT?
questions.explanation: DISTINCT removes duplicate rows from the result set.

options.text: Deletes duplicate rows from the table

questions.title: Purpose of a schema
questions.description: What does a database schema describe?
questions.explanation: A schema defines the structure of the database: tables, columns, types, and the relationships between them.

options.text: The structure and organization of the data
The physical disk where data is stored
The list of currently connected users

questions.title: What LIMIT does
questions.description: What is the purpose of the LIMIT clause?
questions.explanation: LIMIT restricts the number of rows returned by a query.

options.text: Restricts how many rows are returned

questions.title: Row vs column
questions.description: In a table, what does a single row typically represent?
questions.explanation: A row (record) represents a single entity or instance, while columns represent its attributes.

questions.title: Purpose of CREATE TABLE
questions.description: What does the CREATE TABLE statement do?
questions.explanation: CREATE TABLE defines a new table with its columns and data types.

options.text: Defines a new table and its columns
Inserts rows into an existing table

questions.title: What a JOIN does
questions.description: At a high level, what does a SQL JOIN do?
questions.explanation: A JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them.

options.text: Combines rows from multiple tables based on a related column
Appends one table's rows below another's

questions.title: Unique constraint
questions.description: What does a UNIQUE constraint guarantee for a column?
questions.explanation: A UNIQUE constraint ensures no two rows share the same value in that column (NULLs may be allowed depending on the engine).

options.text: No two rows have the same value in that column
The column is automatically indexed and encrypted

questions.title: Purpose of a database
questions.description: What is the core purpose of a database management system (DBMS)?
questions.explanation: A DBMS provides an organized, reliable way to store, retrieve, and manage data with concurrency and integrity controls.

options.text: To store, retrieve, and manage data reliably

questions.title: What GROUP BY does
questions.description: What is the GROUP BY clause used for?
questions.explanation: GROUP BY groups rows that share a value so aggregate functions can be applied per group.

options.text: Groups rows so aggregates can be computed per group

questions.title: Primary key uniqueness
questions.description: Can a primary key column contain duplicate values?
questions.explanation: No. A primary key must be unique across all rows and cannot be NULL.

options.text: No, primary key values must be unique
Only if the table is empty

questions.title: What MAX returns
questions.description: What does the aggregate function MAX(column) return?
questions.explanation: MAX returns the largest value found in the specified column.

options.text: The largest value in the column

questions.title: SQL vs NoSQL basics
questions.description: Which is a defining characteristic of a relational (SQL) database compared to most NoSQL stores?
questions.explanation: Relational databases enforce a predefined schema and support SQL with relationships and joins.

options.text: It uses a predefined schema with tables and relationships
It requires no structure at all

questions.title: What a transaction is
questions.description: At a basic level, what is a database transaction?
questions.explanation: A transaction is a unit of work that is executed as a whole; it either fully completes (commit) or has no effect (rollback).

options.text: A unit of work that fully completes or fully rolls back
A single column in a table

questions.title: Purpose of ALTER TABLE
questions.description: What does ALTER TABLE allow you to do?
questions.explanation: ALTER TABLE changes an existing table's structure, such as adding, modifying, or dropping columns.

options.text: Modify the structure of an existing table

questions.title: What COMMIT does
questions.description: What happens when you COMMIT a transaction?
questions.explanation: COMMIT permanently saves all changes made during the transaction so they become visible to others.

questions.title: What ROLLBACK does
questions.description: What is the effect of a ROLLBACK?
questions.explanation: ROLLBACK undoes all changes made in the current transaction, returning the data to its prior state.

options.text: Undoes the changes made in the current transaction

questions.title: Aggregate SUM
questions.description: What does SUM(column) compute?
questions.explanation: SUM adds up all the numeric values in the specified column across the selected rows.

options.text: The total of all values in the column

questions.title: Default sort direction
questions.description: By default, ORDER BY sorts results in which direction?
questions.explanation: ORDER BY sorts in ascending (ASC) order by default; use DESC for descending.

questions.title: What a column data type defines
questions.description: Why does each column have a defined data type (e.g., INTEGER, VARCHAR)?
questions.explanation: Data types constrain what kind of values a column can store and how they are stored and compared.

options.text: It constrains and describes the kind of values allowed

questions.title: Purpose of AS alias
questions.description: What does the AS keyword do in a SELECT statement?
questions.explanation: AS assigns an alias (alternate name) to a column or table in the result, improving readability.

options.text: Gives a column or table a temporary alias
Casts a value to another type

questions.title: What TRUNCATE does
questions.description: What does the TRUNCATE TABLE statement do?
questions.explanation: TRUNCATE quickly removes all rows from a table while keeping its structure intact.

options.text: Removes all rows but keeps the table structure

questions.title: What a query is
questions.description: In database terms, what is a query?
questions.explanation: A query is a request to retrieve or manipulate data, typically expressed in SQL.

options.text: A request to retrieve or manipulate data

questions.title: AVG aggregate
questions.description: What does the AVG(column) function return?
questions.explanation: AVG returns the arithmetic mean of the numeric values in the column.

options.text: The average of the column's values

questions.title: Purpose of a NOT NULL constraint
questions.description: What does a NOT NULL constraint enforce?
questions.explanation: NOT NULL requires that a column always contain a value; it cannot be left empty/NULL.

options.text: The column must always have a value

questions.title: LIKE operator
questions.description: What is the LIKE operator used for?
questions.explanation: LIKE performs pattern matching on text using wildcards such as % and _.

options.text: Pattern matching on strings with wildcards

questions.title: What a table name must be
questions.description: Within a single schema, table names must be what?
questions.explanation: Table names must be unique within their schema so the database can resolve references unambiguously.

questions.title: Purpose of MIN
questions.description: What does MIN(column) return?
questions.explanation: MIN returns the smallest value found in the specified column.

options.text: The smallest value in the column

questions.title: What IN does
questions.description: What does the IN operator check in a WHERE clause?
questions.explanation: IN tests whether a value matches any value in a given list or subquery result.

options.text: Whether a value is within a specified set of values
Whether a string matches a pattern

questions.title: Purpose of BETWEEN
questions.description: What does the BETWEEN operator do?
questions.explanation: BETWEEN checks whether a value falls within an inclusive range (low AND high).

options.text: Tests if a value falls within an inclusive range

questions.title: What a record is
questions.description: The term 'record' in a database is another word for what?
questions.explanation: A record is another name for a row — a single entry in a table.

questions.title: Auto-increment columns
questions.description: What is the purpose of an auto-increment (identity) column?
questions.explanation: An auto-increment column automatically generates a new sequential number for each inserted row, often used for surrogate primary keys.

options.text: Automatically generate unique sequential values for new rows

questions.title: Purpose of HAVING vs WHERE intro
questions.description: The HAVING clause is most similar in purpose to which other clause?
questions.explanation: HAVING filters results, like WHERE, but it filters groups produced by GROUP BY rather than individual rows.

questions.title: What backup is for
questions.description: Why are database backups important?
questions.explanation: Backups allow data to be restored after accidental loss, corruption, or hardware failure.

options.text: To recover data after loss, corruption, or failure

questions.title: Boolean column
questions.description: A BOOLEAN column typically stores which kind of value?
questions.explanation: A BOOLEAN column stores true/false (and possibly NULL) values.

questions.title: What a view is (intro)
questions.description: At a basic level, what is a database view?
questions.explanation: A view is a saved query that behaves like a virtual table; it does not store data itself but presents data from underlying tables.

options.text: A saved query that acts like a virtual table
A physical copy of a table

questions.title: Purpose of WHERE with DELETE
questions.description: Why is it important to include a WHERE clause with most DELETE statements?
questions.explanation: Without WHERE, DELETE removes every row in the table; WHERE restricts deletion to the intended rows.

options.text: Without it, all rows in the table are deleted
It is required syntax in all databases

questions.title: Composite key (intro)
questions.description: What is a composite primary key?
questions.explanation: A composite primary key is a primary key made up of two or more columns whose combination is unique.

options.text: A primary key made of more than one column
A foreign key pointing to itself

questions.title: Purpose of a DEFAULT value
questions.description: What does a DEFAULT value on a column do?
questions.explanation: A DEFAULT supplies a value for a column when an INSERT does not specify one.

options.text: Provides a value when none is given on insert
Prevents the column from being queried
Makes the column a primary key

questions.title: ACID atomicity
questions.description: In ACID, what does Atomicity guarantee about a transaction?
questions.explanation: Atomicity ensures a transaction is all-or-nothing: either every operation succeeds, or none of them are applied.

options.text: All operations succeed together or none are applied
Data is always stored in RAM
Every query returns the newest data

questions.title: ACID consistency
questions.description: What does the Consistency property in ACID ensure?
questions.explanation: Consistency ensures a transaction moves the database from one valid state to another, preserving all defined rules and constraints.

options.text: The database remains in a valid state, respecting constraints
Data is replicated to all nodes instantly

questions.title: ACID isolation
questions.description: What does Isolation in ACID concern?
questions.explanation: Isolation ensures concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other; results should be as if transactions ran serially.

options.text: How concurrent transactions are kept from interfering
How data is encrypted on disk

questions.title: ACID durability
questions.description: What does Durability guarantee in ACID?
questions.explanation: Durability ensures that once a transaction is committed, its changes survive even a crash or power loss.

options.text: Committed changes persist even after a crash
Transactions can always be rolled back

questions.title: INNER JOIN result
questions.description: What rows does an INNER JOIN return?
questions.explanation: An INNER JOIN returns only rows that have matching values in both joined tables.

options.text: Only rows with matches in both tables
All rows from the left table
Rows with no match in either table

questions.title: LEFT JOIN behavior
questions.description: What does a LEFT (OUTER) JOIN return?
questions.explanation: A LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matching rows from the right; unmatched right-side columns are NULL.

options.text: All left-table rows, with NULLs where the right has no match

questions.title: First normal form
questions.description: What does First Normal Form (1NF) require?
questions.explanation: 1NF requires that each column hold atomic (indivisible) values and that there are no repeating groups or arrays in a column.

options.text: Each column holds atomic values with no repeating groups
Every table has a foreign key

questions.title: Second normal form
questions.description: What additional rule does Second Normal Form (2NF) add over 1NF?
questions.explanation: 2NF requires 1NF plus that every non-key column depends on the whole primary key, eliminating partial dependencies on part of a composite key.

options.text: No partial dependency on part of a composite key
Every column must be NOT NULL

questions.title: Third normal form
questions.description: What does Third Normal Form (3NF) eliminate?
questions.explanation: 3NF requires 2NF plus removing transitive dependencies, so non-key columns depend only on the primary key, not on other non-key columns.

options.text: The need for a primary key

questions.title: Purpose of normalization
questions.description: What is the main goal of normalizing a relational schema?
questions.explanation: Normalization reduces data redundancy and avoids update anomalies by organizing data into related tables.

options.text: Reduce redundancy and prevent update anomalies

questions.title: Denormalization trade-off
questions.description: Why might a team deliberately denormalize a schema?
questions.explanation: Denormalization adds controlled redundancy to reduce expensive joins and speed up reads, at the cost of more complex writes and potential inconsistency.

options.text: To speed up reads by avoiding costly joins
To guarantee no data is ever duplicated

questions.title: What an index costs
questions.description: What is a typical downside of adding many indexes to a table?
questions.explanation: Indexes must be updated on every write, so they slow down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and consume extra storage.

options.text: Slower writes and more storage usage

questions.title: B-tree index use case
questions.description: A B-tree index is especially well suited for which kind of query?
questions.explanation: B-tree indexes efficiently support equality and range queries (e.g., <, >, BETWEEN) and ordered scans.

options.text: Equality and range lookups on ordered data

questions.title: HAVING clause role
questions.description: When is the HAVING clause evaluated relative to GROUP BY?
questions.explanation: HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY has aggregated rows, allowing conditions on aggregate values like COUNT or SUM.

options.text: After grouping, to filter aggregated groups
After ORDER BY sorts the rows

questions.title: Subquery definition
questions.description: What is a subquery (nested query)?
questions.explanation: A subquery is a query nested inside another query, whose result is used by the outer query.

options.text: A query embedded within another query
A query that runs on a backup
A query without a WHERE clause
A query that returns no rows

questions.title: Correlated subquery
questions.description: What distinguishes a correlated subquery from a regular subquery?
questions.explanation: A correlated subquery references columns from the outer query and is re-evaluated for each outer row.

options.text: It references the outer query and runs per outer row
It always returns a single constant

questions.title: Connection pooling purpose
questions.description: Why do applications use a database connection pool?
questions.explanation: Opening connections is expensive; a pool reuses a set of established connections to reduce latency and limit concurrent load on the database.

options.text: To reuse established connections and limit overhead

questions.title: Read replica purpose
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of a read replica?
questions.explanation: Read replicas copy data from a primary to offload read traffic, improving read scalability and availability.

options.text: To serve read queries and offload the primary

questions.title: Primary-replica write path
questions.description: In a typical primary-replica setup, where are writes directed?
questions.explanation: Writes go to the primary, which then replicates changes to the read replicas.

options.text: To the primary, which replicates to replicas
To all nodes simultaneously by the client

questions.title: Replication lag
questions.description: What is replication lag?
questions.explanation: Replication lag is the delay between a write committing on the primary and that change appearing on a replica, which can cause stale reads.

options.text: The delay before a replica reflects the primary's writes
The time to open a connection
The size of the transaction log
The latency of an index rebuild

questions.title: What sharding is
questions.description: What does horizontal sharding do to a dataset?
questions.explanation: Sharding partitions rows across multiple database nodes by a shard key, so each node holds a subset of the data.

options.text: Splits rows across multiple nodes by a shard key
Copies the whole dataset to every node

questions.title: Stored procedure
questions.description: What is a stored procedure?
questions.explanation: A stored procedure is a precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database and executed by name, often with parameters.

options.text: Saved SQL logic stored and executed in the database

questions.title: Database trigger
questions.description: What is a database trigger?
questions.explanation: A trigger is code that automatically executes in response to events such as INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE on a table.

options.text: Code that runs automatically on data-change events

questions.title: UNION vs UNION ALL
questions.description: What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
questions.explanation: UNION removes duplicate rows from the combined result; UNION ALL keeps all rows including duplicates and is faster.

options.text: UNION removes duplicates; UNION ALL keeps them
UNION ALL sorts results; UNION does not
UNION joins tables side by side

questions.title: What an EXPLAIN plan shows
questions.description: What does running EXPLAIN on a query reveal?
questions.explanation: EXPLAIN shows the query planner's execution strategy, including join types, index usage, and estimated costs.

options.text: How the database plans to execute the query
The raw bytes of the table on disk

questions.title: Full table scan meaning
questions.description: What does a full table scan indicate in a query plan?
questions.explanation: A full table scan reads every row in the table, often because no suitable index exists; it can be slow on large tables.

options.text: The database reads every row, often missing an index
The query used an index efficiently

questions.title: Composite index column order
questions.description: Why does column order matter in a composite (multi-column) index?
questions.explanation: A composite index is most useful when queries filter on a leftmost prefix of its columns; order determines which queries can use it.

options.text: Queries benefit only when filtering a leftmost prefix of columns
Only the last column is used
It changes the table's row order

questions.title: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking
questions.description: What characterizes optimistic locking?
questions.explanation: Optimistic locking assumes conflicts are rare; it checks at commit time (e.g., via a version column) and retries if a conflict is detected, without holding locks.

options.text: It detects conflicts at commit time instead of locking upfront
It locks rows for the entire transaction

questions.title: Deadlock definition
questions.description: What is a database deadlock?
questions.explanation: A deadlock occurs when two or more transactions each hold a lock the other needs, so none can proceed; the database usually aborts one to break it.

options.text: Two transactions each waiting on a lock the other holds
A query that runs forever due to a missing index

questions.title: Surrogate key
questions.description: What is a surrogate key?
questions.explanation: A surrogate key is an artificial, system-generated identifier (like an auto-increment ID or UUID) with no business meaning, used as the primary key.

options.text: A system-generated identifier with no business meaning
A key derived from real-world data like email
A foreign key to another database
A temporary key for a session

questions.title: Natural key
questions.description: What is a natural key?
questions.explanation: A natural key is a primary key formed from existing, meaningful business data (such as an ISBN or email), rather than a generated value.

options.text: A primary key based on meaningful real-world data

questions.title: CHECK constraint
questions.description: What does a CHECK constraint do?
questions.explanation: A CHECK constraint enforces that a column's values satisfy a boolean expression, e.g., price > 0.

options.text: Enforces that values meet a specified condition

questions.title: ON DELETE CASCADE
questions.description: What happens with a foreign key defined as ON DELETE CASCADE?
questions.explanation: When the referenced parent row is deleted, the child rows that reference it are automatically deleted too.

options.text: Deleting the parent row also deletes the related child rows
Deletion of the parent is blocked
Child rows are set to NULL

questions.title: Materialized view
questions.description: How does a materialized view differ from a regular view?
questions.explanation: A materialized view stores the query result physically and must be refreshed, whereas a regular view computes results on each access.

options.text: It stores results physically and needs refreshing
It is always faster to write to

questions.title: Transaction isolation: dirty read
questions.description: What is a dirty read?
questions.explanation: A dirty read occurs when a transaction reads data written by another transaction that has not yet committed and might be rolled back.

options.text: Reading uncommitted changes from another transaction
Reading from a corrupted disk block

questions.title: Primary key vs unique index
questions.description: How does a primary key differ from a plain unique index?
questions.explanation: A primary key is a unique constraint that also forbids NULLs and serves as the table's main identifier; a table has at most one primary key but can have many unique indexes.

options.text: A primary key disallows NULLs and there is only one per table
Unique indexes disallow NULLs but primary keys allow them
A table can have many primary keys

questions.title: Cartesian product / CROSS JOIN
questions.description: What does a CROSS JOIN produce?
questions.explanation: A CROSS JOIN produces the Cartesian product: every row of the first table paired with every row of the second.

options.text: Every combination of rows from both tables
Rows from the left table only

questions.title: GROUP BY with aggregates
questions.description: When using GROUP BY, what must be true of non-aggregated columns in the SELECT list (in standard SQL)?
questions.explanation: In standard SQL, every selected column that is not inside an aggregate must appear in the GROUP BY clause.

options.text: They must appear in the GROUP BY clause

questions.title: Covering index
questions.description: What is a covering index?
questions.explanation: A covering index includes all columns a query needs, so the query can be answered from the index alone without reading the table rows.

options.text: An index that contains all columns a query needs
An index on every column of a table

questions.title: Cardinality and index usefulness
questions.description: How does a column's cardinality affect index usefulness?
questions.explanation: High-cardinality columns (many distinct values) make indexes more selective and useful; low-cardinality columns yield less benefit.

options.text: High cardinality makes an index more selective and useful
Low cardinality always makes the best indexes
Cardinality has no effect on indexes

questions.title: What WAL is for
questions.description: What is the purpose of a write-ahead log (WAL)?
questions.explanation: A WAL records changes before they are applied to the data files, enabling crash recovery and durability.

options.text: Record changes before applying them, enabling recovery

questions.title: Eventual consistency
questions.description: What does eventual consistency mean?
questions.explanation: With eventual consistency, replicas may temporarily diverge but will converge to the same value if no new writes occur.

options.text: Replicas converge to the same state over time
All reads are always immediately up to date

questions.title: OLTP vs OLAP
questions.description: Which workload best describes OLTP systems?
questions.explanation: OLTP (online transaction processing) handles many short, write-heavy transactions, while OLAP focuses on complex analytical reads.

questions.title: Foreign key and orphan rows
questions.description: How does a foreign key constraint help prevent orphan rows?
questions.explanation: A foreign key blocks inserting a child row that references a non-existent parent, preventing orphaned references.

options.text: It rejects child rows referencing a missing parent
It deletes all child rows automatically

questions.title: COALESCE function
questions.description: What does COALESCE(a, b, c) return?
questions.explanation: COALESCE returns the first non-NULL argument from its list, commonly used to provide default values.

options.text: The first non-NULL value in the list
NULL if any argument is NULL

questions.title: Self join
questions.description: What is a self join?
questions.explanation: A self join joins a table to itself, typically using aliases, to relate rows within the same table (e.g., employees to their managers).

options.text: Joining a table to itself using aliases
A join that returns no rows

questions.title: Index on low-selectivity boolean
questions.description: Why is a plain B-tree index on a low-selectivity boolean column often not helpful?
questions.explanation: When a column has few distinct values, each value matches many rows, so the planner may prefer a full scan over the index.

options.text: Each value matches too many rows to be selective

questions.title: Primary vs foreign key direction
questions.description: A foreign key in a child table typically references what in the parent table?
questions.explanation: A foreign key references the primary key (or a unique key) of the parent table.

options.text: The parent table's primary or unique key

questions.title: What VACUUM/compaction addresses
questions.description: In MVCC databases, what problem does a process like VACUUM (or compaction) address?
questions.explanation: MVCC keeps old row versions for concurrent readers; VACUUM reclaims space from dead tuples that are no longer visible to any transaction.

options.text: Reclaiming space from obsolete row versions

questions.title: Pagination with OFFSET cost
questions.description: Why can OFFSET-based pagination become slow on deep pages?
questions.explanation: OFFSET still scans and discards all skipped rows, so deep offsets read progressively more rows; keyset (seek) pagination avoids this by filtering on the last seen key.

options.text: The database must scan and skip all rows before the offset

questions.title: Transaction isolation: serializable
questions.description: What does the SERIALIZABLE isolation level guarantee?
questions.explanation: SERIALIZABLE guarantees that concurrent transactions produce a result equivalent to some serial (one-at-a-time) ordering, preventing all anomalies including phantom reads.

options.text: Results equivalent to running transactions one at a time
Reads of uncommitted data are allowed

questions.title: Phantom read
questions.description: What is a phantom read?
questions.explanation: A phantom read happens when a transaction re-runs a range query and sees new rows inserted by another committed transaction that match the predicate.

options.text: A repeated range query returns newly inserted matching rows
Reading a row that no longer exists physically
A read from a stale cache

questions.title: Non-repeatable read
questions.description: What is a non-repeatable read?
questions.explanation: A non-repeatable read occurs when a transaction reads the same row twice and gets different values because another committed transaction modified it in between.

options.text: The same row yields different values on re-read within a transaction
A query that cannot be run twice
Reading from two tables at once

questions.title: MVCC mechanism
questions.description: How does Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC) let readers avoid blocking writers?
questions.explanation: MVCC keeps multiple versions of a row so readers see a consistent snapshot from their transaction's start, while writers create new versions without blocking those readers.

options.text: Readers see a snapshot version while writers create new versions
Writes are queued until all reads finish
Only one transaction runs at a time

questions.title: Two-phase commit
questions.description: What problem does the two-phase commit (2PC) protocol solve?
questions.explanation: 2PC coordinates an atomic commit across multiple nodes/resources: a prepare phase followed by a commit phase ensures all participants commit or all abort.

options.text: Atomic commit of a transaction across multiple nodes

questions.title: 2PC blocking weakness
questions.description: What is a well-known weakness of two-phase commit?
questions.explanation: If the coordinator fails after participants have prepared, they may block indefinitely holding locks while awaiting the final decision.

options.text: Participants can block holding locks if the coordinator fails
It only works on a single machine
It removes the need for a WAL

questions.title: Saga pattern
questions.description: What does the saga pattern use instead of distributed locks to maintain consistency across services?
questions.explanation: A saga breaks a distributed transaction into a series of local transactions, each with a compensating action to undo prior steps on failure.

options.text: A sequence of local transactions with compensating actions
A global lock across all services
A single ACID transaction across services

questions.title: Write amplification in LSM trees
questions.description: What causes write amplification in an LSM-tree storage engine?
questions.explanation: Background compaction repeatedly rewrites data as it merges SSTables across levels, so a single logical write results in multiple physical writes.

options.text: Compaction rewrites data multiple times across levels

questions.title: LSM tree read trade-off
questions.description: Compared to B-trees, what is a typical read-side trade-off of LSM-tree engines?
questions.explanation: LSM trees optimize writes but a read may need to check several SSTables (and bloom filters) across levels, potentially increasing read amplification.

options.text: Reads may need to consult multiple SSTables (read amplification)
Reads are always faster than B-trees

questions.title: Bloom filter in databases
questions.description: How do storage engines use a bloom filter to speed up reads?
questions.explanation: A bloom filter probabilistically reports whether a key is definitely absent from an SSTable, letting the engine skip files that cannot contain the key (with possible false positives, never false negatives).

options.text: To skip files that definitely do not contain a key
To guarantee a key exists in a file

questions.title: Quorum reads and writes
questions.description: In a quorum system with N replicas, what condition on read quorum R and write quorum W guarantees strong consistency?
questions.explanation: When R + W > N, every read quorum overlaps with the latest write quorum, so a read always sees the most recent write.

questions.title: Hash sharding hotspots
questions.description: Why does hash-based sharding generally avoid range-scan hotspots better than range sharding?
questions.explanation: Hashing distributes sequential or skewed keys uniformly across shards, preventing a single shard from receiving a burst of adjacent-key traffic — at the cost of efficient range scans.

options.text: Hashing spreads adjacent keys evenly across shards
Hashing keeps adjacent keys on one shard
Hashing eliminates the need for a shard key

questions.title: Consistent hashing benefit
questions.description: What problem does consistent hashing primarily solve in a sharded/distributed store?
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing minimizes the number of keys that must move when nodes are added or removed, avoiding a full reshuffle.

options.text: Minimizing key remapping when nodes are added or removed
Removing the need for a hash function

questions.title: Index-only scan requirement
questions.description: For an index-only scan to be used in PostgreSQL, what must generally be true?
questions.explanation: All columns the query needs must be in the index, and the visibility map must show the pages as all-visible so heap lookups can be skipped.

options.text: The index covers all needed columns and pages are all-visible
The table has no primary key
The query uses no WHERE clause
The index is on a boolean column

questions.title: Snapshot isolation anomaly
questions.description: Which anomaly can occur under snapshot isolation but not under true serializable isolation?
questions.explanation: Write skew can occur under snapshot isolation: two transactions read overlapping data and make disjoint writes that together violate a constraint, which serializable isolation would prevent.

questions.title: Gap locks purpose
questions.description: In databases like MySQL's InnoDB, what is the purpose of a gap lock?
questions.explanation: Gap locks lock the space between index records to prevent other transactions from inserting into that range, which blocks phantom rows under repeatable read.

options.text: To prevent inserts into a range, blocking phantoms

questions.title: Synchronous vs asynchronous replication
questions.description: What is the key durability trade-off of asynchronous replication?
questions.explanation: Asynchronous replication acknowledges a write before replicas confirm it, so a primary failure can lose recently committed writes that never reached a replica.

options.text: Recent committed writes can be lost if the primary fails
Writes always wait for every replica

questions.title: Split-brain in failover
questions.description: What is a split-brain scenario in a replicated database cluster?
questions.explanation: Split-brain occurs when a network partition causes two nodes to each believe they are the primary, accepting conflicting writes.

options.text: Two nodes both act as primary and accept conflicting writes
The cluster runs out of disk
An index is built on two columns

questions.title: Fencing tokens
questions.description: How do fencing tokens prevent a stale leader from corrupting data after failover?
questions.explanation: A monotonically increasing fencing token is attached to each request; resources reject any request carrying a lower token than one already seen, blocking an outdated leader.

options.text: Resources reject requests with an out-of-date (lower) token
They speed up the new leader

questions.title: Covering index vs heap fetch
questions.description: Why can a covering index dramatically speed up a query?
questions.explanation: Because the index holds every column the query needs, the engine avoids random heap/row lookups, turning many scattered reads into a single ordered index scan.

options.text: It avoids extra random row (heap) lookups
It removes the WHERE clause cost

questions.title: Skewed shard key
questions.description: What is the main risk of choosing a low-cardinality or skewed shard key?
questions.explanation: A skewed shard key concentrates data and traffic on a few shards (hotspots), undermining the scalability benefit of sharding.

options.text: Hotspots where a few shards hold most data and load

questions.title: Idempotency in retries
questions.description: Why is idempotency important when an application retries a database write after a timeout?
questions.explanation: After a timeout the client cannot know if the write applied; an idempotent operation (e.g., keyed by a unique request id) ensures retries do not duplicate effects.

options.text: It ensures retries do not apply the write twice
It removes the need for transactions

questions.title: CAP under partition
questions.description: Per the CAP theorem, during a network partition a distributed database must sacrifice which property if it stays available?
questions.explanation: During a partition you can keep availability or consistency but not both; an AP system sacrifices strong consistency to remain available.

questions.title: PACELC extension
questions.description: What does the PACELC theorem add beyond CAP?
questions.explanation: PACELC states that besides the partition (P) trade-off of availability vs consistency, Else (E) — when the system is running normally — there is a trade-off between latency (L) and consistency (C).

options.text: Even without partitions, there is a latency vs consistency trade-off

questions.title: Hot vs cold backup
questions.description: What distinguishes a hot backup from a cold backup?
questions.explanation: A hot backup is taken while the database is online and serving traffic; a cold backup requires the database to be stopped/offline.

options.text: A hot backup runs while the database is online
A hot backup requires the database to be offline
A cold backup is always incremental

questions.title: Point-in-time recovery
questions.description: What enables point-in-time recovery (PITR)?
questions.explanation: PITR combines a base backup with the continuous transaction/WAL log so the database can be replayed to any chosen moment before a failure.

options.text: A base backup plus continuous WAL/transaction logs to replay
Only a single nightly full dump
A read replica with no logs

questions.title: N+1 query problem
questions.description: What is the N+1 query problem?
questions.explanation: It occurs when code runs one query to fetch a list and then one additional query per item to fetch related data, producing N+1 round trips instead of a single join or batched query.

options.text: Running one query per item instead of batching, causing many round trips
A query that returns one extra row

questions.title: Index selectivity and planner
questions.description: Why might a query planner choose a sequential scan even when a usable index exists?
questions.explanation: If the predicate matches a large fraction of rows, the planner estimates that scanning sequentially is cheaper than many random index lookups.

options.text: When the query matches enough rows that a scan is cheaper
Indexes are never used by planners
Because sequential scans are always faster

questions.title: Lock escalation
questions.description: What is lock escalation?
questions.explanation: Lock escalation converts many fine-grained locks (e.g., row locks) into a single coarser lock (e.g., a table lock) to reduce lock-management overhead, at the cost of concurrency.

options.text: Converting many fine-grained locks into one coarse lock

questions.title: Foreign key indexing pitfall
questions.description: Why is it often recommended to index the foreign key column on the child side?
questions.explanation: Without an index on the child's foreign key, operations like deleting or updating a parent key can trigger slow full scans of the child table to check references.

options.text: To avoid slow child-table scans during parent updates/deletes
Because foreign keys cannot exist without an index

questions.title: Read-your-writes consistency
questions.description: What does read-your-writes consistency guarantee?
questions.explanation: Read-your-writes ensures that after a client makes a write, that same client's subsequent reads will reflect the write, even if other clients might not yet.

options.text: A client always sees its own prior writes on later reads
All clients see all writes instantly
Writes are never visible to the writer

questions.title: Partial index
questions.description: What is a partial index and when is it useful?
questions.explanation: A partial index indexes only rows satisfying a predicate (e.g., WHERE active = true), saving space and speeding queries that target that subset.

options.text: An index over only a subset of rows matching a condition
An index on half of each value
An index that is rebuilt every night

questions.title: Clustered vs non-clustered index
questions.description: How does a clustered index differ from a non-clustered index?
questions.explanation: A clustered index determines the physical order of the table's rows (the data is the index leaf), so a table can have only one; non-clustered indexes are separate structures pointing to rows.

options.text: A clustered index defines the physical row order; only one per table
A clustered index is always smaller
Non-clustered indexes store the full rows

questions.title: Change data capture
questions.description: What is Change Data Capture (CDC) primarily used for?
questions.explanation: CDC streams row-level changes (often from the transaction log) so downstream systems like search indexes, caches, or data warehouses stay in sync.

options.text: Streaming row changes to keep other systems in sync

questions.title: Isolation level default trade-off
questions.description: Why do many databases default to READ COMMITTED rather than SERIALIZABLE?
questions.explanation: READ COMMITTED offers good concurrency and performance while preventing dirty reads; SERIALIZABLE adds safety but increases blocking, aborts, and overhead.

options.text: It balances concurrency and performance against stricter guarantees
It allows dirty reads for speed
It is the only level that supports indexes

questions.title: Hot partition mitigation
questions.description: Which technique helps mitigate a hot partition caused by a sequential key like a timestamp?
questions.explanation: Adding a hash prefix or salt to the partition key spreads sequential writes across multiple partitions, relieving the hotspot.

options.text: Prefixing/salting the key to spread writes across partitions

questions.title: Foreign key vs application-level integrity
questions.description: What is a trade-off of enforcing referential integrity in the application instead of with database foreign keys?
questions.explanation: Application-level enforcement can scale across sharded/distributed stores that lack cross-node FKs, but it risks inconsistency if any code path or concurrent write bypasses the check.

options.text: More flexibility but higher risk of inconsistent data
It is always faster and safer
It removes the need for primary keys
It guarantees stronger integrity than FKs

questions.title: Leader election in distributed DBs
questions.description: What is the role of a consensus algorithm like Raft in a distributed database?
questions.explanation: Raft (or Paxos) lets a cluster agree on a single leader and a consistent, ordered log of operations despite failures, underpinning consistent replication.

options.text: Agreeing on a leader and a consistent ordered log of operations

questions.title: Tombstones in distributed stores
questions.description: What is a tombstone in a log-structured or distributed store, and why can it cause problems?
questions.explanation: A tombstone is a marker that a key was deleted; it must propagate to all replicas before being purged, and accumulating tombstones can slow reads and range scans.

options.text: A delete marker that must replicate and can degrade read performance

questions.title: Connection pool exhaustion
questions.description: What symptom typically indicates database connection pool exhaustion under load?
questions.explanation: When all pooled connections are in use, new requests wait for a free connection and may time out, even though the database itself has spare capacity.

options.text: Requests block waiting for a free connection and time out

questions.title: Write skew example
questions.description: Which scenario is a classic example of write skew?
questions.explanation: Two on-call doctors each check that at least one other doctor is on duty and both go off-call simultaneously under snapshot isolation, violating the constraint that one must remain.

options.text: Two transactions read a shared invariant then make disjoint writes that break it
A single transaction updating one row twice

questions.title: Index bloat
questions.description: What is index bloat and how does it commonly arise?
questions.explanation: Index bloat is wasted space from dead entries left by many updates/deletes under MVCC; it inflates index size and slows scans until the index is rebuilt or vacuumed.

options.text: Wasted index space from dead entries after many updates/deletes
An index that is too small to use
An index without a primary key

questions.title: Logical vs physical replication
questions.description: How does logical replication differ from physical (binary) replication?
questions.explanation: Logical replication ships row-level change events that can be filtered and applied across versions or selected tables; physical replication copies the exact byte-level WAL/blocks of the whole cluster.

options.text: Logical ships row-change events; physical copies byte-level WAL
Logical replication requires identical disk layouts
Physical replication can filter individual tables easily

questions.title: Skip-locked for queue tables
questions.description: Why is SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED useful for a job-queue table?
questions.explanation: SKIP LOCKED lets concurrent workers grab different unlocked rows without blocking on rows another worker already locked, enabling parallel queue consumption.

options.text: Workers grab different rows concurrently without blocking each other
It locks the entire table for one worker

questions.title: Denormalization and write anomalies
questions.description: What is the primary risk introduced by denormalizing data into multiple places?
questions.explanation: Duplicated data must be kept in sync on every write; missing an update path leads to inconsistent copies (update anomalies).

options.text: Copies can drift out of sync if not all are updated
The schema can no longer be queried

questions.title: Time-series partitioning
questions.description: Why is time-based range partitioning common for large time-series tables?
questions.explanation: Partitioning by time lets queries prune to relevant partitions and makes dropping old data a fast partition-drop instead of a slow bulk DELETE.

options.text: It enables partition pruning and fast drops of old data
It removes the need for indexes

questions.title: Foreign key locking under concurrency
questions.description: How can foreign key constraints contribute to contention under high concurrency?
questions.explanation: Inserting/updating a child row takes a shared lock on the referenced parent row to ensure it still exists, which can serialize transactions touching popular parent rows.

options.text: They acquire locks on referenced parent rows, serializing some writes

questions.title: Query planner statistics staleness
questions.description: Why can stale planner statistics lead to poor query plans?
questions.explanation: The planner estimates row counts from collected statistics; if they are outdated after large data changes, it may misjudge selectivity and pick an inefficient plan.

options.text: The planner misestimates row counts and picks a bad plan
They have no effect on planning

questions.title: Append-only design benefit
questions.description: What is a key benefit of an append-only (immutable) table design?
questions.explanation: Append-only writes avoid in-place updates and locks on existing rows, simplifying concurrency, enabling full history/audit, and pairing well with log-structured storage.

options.text: Simpler concurrency and a full audit history of changes
It removes the need for storage

questions.title: HOT updates in PostgreSQL
questions.description: What condition allows a PostgreSQL Heap-Only Tuple (HOT) update, and why is it beneficial?
questions.explanation: A HOT update applies when no indexed column changes and the new row version fits on the same page; it avoids creating new index entries, reducing index bloat and write cost.

options.text: No indexed column changes and the new version fits on the same page
Any update where a primary key changes
Only updates inside a serializable transaction
Updates that move the row to another partition
